back_translation: Later, after these events, King Ahasuerus promoted Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite; he exalted him and set his seat above all the officials who were with him.

All the king's servants stationed at the palace gate would bow down and prostrate themselves before Haman, because the king had commanded that it be done so. But Mordecai would not bow down or prostrate himself.
So the king's servants at the palace gate asked Mordecai, 'Why do you transgress the king's command?'
When they warned him day after day but he would not listen, they reported it to Haman to see whether Mordecai's claim would stand, since he had told them he was a Jew.
When Haman saw that Mordecai would not bow down or prostrate himself before him, Haman was filled with rage.

But he regarded laying hands on Mordecai alone as too small a thing; since they had told him what people Mordecai belonged to, Haman sought a way to destroy all the Jews — Mordecai's people — throughout the kingdom of Ahasuerus.

In the first month, the month of Nisan, in the twelfth year of King Ahasuerus' reign, the Pur (that is, the lot) was cast before Haman to find a day and a month, casting day by day and month by month, until it fell on the twelfth month, the month of Adar.

Then Haman said to King Ahasuerus, 'There is a certain people scattered and dispersed among the peoples in every province of your kingdom; their customary laws differ from those of every other people, and they do not obey the king's laws, so it is not fitting for the king to tolerate them.
If it pleases the king, let a decree be written to destroy them, and I will weigh out ten thousand talents of silver to the officials who carry it out, to bring into the royal treasury.'
So the king removed the signet ring from his hand and gave it to Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite, the enemy of the Jews.
The king said to Haman, 'The silver is given to you, and the people as well, to do with as you see fit.'

On the thirteenth day of the first month, the royal scribes were summoned, and a decree was written according to all that Haman commanded, to the king's satraps, the governors over each province, and the officials of each people, in the script of each province and the language of each people; it was written in the name of King Ahasuerus and sealed with the king's signet ring.

The letters were sent by swift couriers to all the provinces of the king, commanding to destroy, kill, and annihilate all the Jews — young and old, children and women — in a single day, the thirteenth day of the twelfth month, the month of Adar, and to plunder their goods.
A copy of the decree was to be issued as law in every province and published to all peoples, so that everyone would be ready for that day.

The couriers went out in haste by the king's command, and the decree was proclaimed in the citadel-city of Susa. The king and Haman sat down to drink together, but the whole city of Susa was in confusion.
